JavaScript

Web Development

Programming

Frontend Development

Coding

3 min read
Just now

--

Reading Time: 3 min
Introduction
If you’ve just started learning JavaScript, you’ve probably written code like this:
let name = "Dhruvil";
console.log(name);It works.
But…
Have you ever wondered what actually happens after you press Enter?
Where does the variable go?
How does JavaScript remember it?
Why do some variables exist while others suddenly become undefined?
Understanding these questions is what separates someone who can write JavaScript from someone who truly understands it.
This article starts there.
A Simple Analogy
Imagine your computer is a huge office building.
Every time JavaScript runs, it opens a brand-new room.
Inside that room, it keeps track of:

• Variables

• Functions

• Objects

• Memory
That’s called the Execution Context.
Think of it as JavaScript’s workspace.
Let’s See It
let username = "Dhruvil";
let age = 23;console.log(username);
console.log(age);
When this code runs, JavaScript stores something like this internally:
Memoryusername → "Dhruvil"
age → 23
When you ask for username, JavaScript simply looks it up and returns the stored value.
Challenge #1
Without running the code, predict the output.
let city = "Rajkot";console.log(city);
city = "Ahmedabad";
console.log(city);
Write your answer before opening your editor.
Answer
Rajkot
AhmedabadWhy?
Because variables can be reassigned.
A Common Mistake
const pi = 3.14;pi = 3.14159;
What happens?
JavaScript throws an error because const creates a binding that cannot be reassigned.
Notice that this doesn’t mean every value inside a const object is frozen—that's a different concept you'll learn later.
Memorypi
↓
3.14
❌ Cannot point somewhere else
Mini Exercise
Create three variables:

• Your name

• Your favorite framework

• Years of coding experience
Print them in one sentence using template literals.
Example output:
Hi, I'm Dhruvil. I enjoy React and I've been coding for 2 years.Stretch Challenge
Create a small profile object:
const profile = {
name: "",
skills: [],
location: ""
};Fill it with your own values and log a sentence using those properties.
What You Learned

• JavaScript stores values in memory.

• Variables are names that point to those values.

• let variables can be reassigned.

• const variables cannot be reassigned.
What’s Next?
In the next chapter, you’ll learn how JavaScript decides where a variable belongs, which leads into scope and why some variables are available while others are not.

Bonus Section
Interview Corner
This series isn’t just about learning JavaScript — it’s also about preparing you for real-world frontend interviews.
Interview Question
What is the difference between let, const, and var?
Before you search online or jump into the next article, take a moment to think about it.
Ask yourself:

• Which one is block-scoped?

• Which one can be reassigned?

• Which one is hoisted?

• Why is var generally avoided in modern JavaScript?
Challenge: Write down your answer in your own words before moving on.
We’ll explore each of these concepts with visual diagrams, practical examples, and common interview scenarios in the next chapter.
